title: Spawning and Managing Tasks
content:
tokio::spawn() creates a new async task that runs concurrently with other tasks. A spawned task is like a lightweight thread managed by the runtime. Tasks are multiplexed onto a thread pool.

Spawned tasks return a JoinHandle that you can .await to get the task's result. If a task panics, the JoinHandle returns an error rather than crashing the whole program.

Important constraint: spawned tasks must be 'static -- they cannot borrow data from the spawning scope. This is because the task may outlive the scope that spawned it. Use Arc for shared ownership or clone data into the task.

For structured concurrency (waiting on multiple tasks), use tokio::join!, tokio::try_join!, or JoinSet.
keyPoints: tokio::spawn() creates a concurrent task on the runtime's thread pool
Spawned tasks must be 'static -- use Arc or move data in
JoinHandle lets you await a task's result or detect panics
tokio::join! runs multiple futures concurrently and waits for all
JoinSet manages a dynamic collection of spawned tasks
Tasks are much lighter than OS threads -- spawn thousands without issue
